channel.title: Kailen A. Mooney on DICE Lab: Diversity In Changing Environments
channel.description: Recent content in Kailen A. Mooney on DICE Lab: Diversity In Changing Environments

item.title: Elevational gradients in plant defences and insect herbivory: recent advances in the field and prospects for future research
item.description: Abstract Classic research on elevational gradients in plant-herbivore interactions holds that insect herbivore pressure is stronger under the warmer, less seasonal climates characteristic of low elevations, and that this in turn selects for increased defence in low- (relative to high-) elevation plants.

item.title: Sex-specific responses to climate change in plants alter population sex ratio and performance
item.description: Abstract Males and females are ecologically distinct in many species, but whether responses to climate change are sex-specific is unknown.

item.title: Trade-offs between constitutive and induced defences drive geographical and climatic clines in pine chemical defences
item.description: Abstract There is increasing evidence that geographic and climatic clines drive the patterns of plant defence allocation and defensive strategies.

item.title: Mechanisms underlying plant sexual dimorphism in multi-trophic arthropod communities
item.description: Abstract A growing body of research documents the importance of plant genetic effects on arthropod community structure.

item.title: Plant sex and induced responses independently influence herbivore performance, natural enemies and aphid-tending ants
item.description: Abstract Sex is an ecologically important form of genetic variation in dioecious plants, with males and females generally differing in constitutive resistance to herbivores.

item.title: Influence of macronutrient imbalance on native ant foraging and interspecific interactions in the field
item.description: Abstract Ants interact with a diversity of organisms.
